### Instruction:
What is Orchesis - either professional or amateur

### Response:
generated_text:
Orchesis is the art of theatrical production. It may refer to one of two distinct theatrical producing professions in the modern world, but its original and longer meaning was exclusively the work done by professionally trained stage managers in orchestras.

In the 18th century, opera was a genre of live musical performance for a paying audience. Stage directors, also known as 'orchestrators', organized the sets, costumes, lights and props on which the operatic singers worked. They were responsible not only for the artistic vision expressed through each element of the production, but also for ensuring that every aspect of the production worked seamlessly with all other elements.  Because they collaborated extensively with the engineers, designers and builders involved in bringing the spectacle of the opera to life, they became known informally as "opera technicians" or even more properly as "theatre technicus".

The term "orchestral director" was coined primarily during the 19th century, although some classical composers used the term orchestral director in their own time (Georges Bizet, for example, specified in his Carmen op. 27 that the person appointed to direct both the singing and acting parts should be called diretor d'orchestra). Even today in popular culture, orchestration is frequently equated with the career of a music conductor; indeed it can sometimes mean simply that a musician has been hired to lead an orchestra on stage. However an orchestra conductor's major duty is generally only sound coaching, facilitating the performances offered by the artists who play the instruments, without assuming any significant responsibilities for the overall direction of the musical output (except possibly as creative consultant/co-creator)

What is measure of inflation in the US?

### Input:
In economics, inflation is an increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy.[3][4][5][6] When the general price level rises, each unit of currency buys fewer goods and services; consequently, inflation corresponds to a reduction in the purchasing power of money.[7][8] The opposite of inflation is deflation, a decrease in the general price level of goods and services. The common measure of inflation is the inflation rate, the annualized percentage change in a general price index.[9] As prices faced by households do not all increase at the same rate, the consumer price index (CPI) is often used for this purpose. The employment cost index is also used for wages in the United States.

### Instruction:
Given the following paragraph on cell structure, what is an example of membrane proteins?

### Input:
Every cell is enclosed within a cell membrane that separates its cytoplasm from the extracellular space.[41] A cell membrane consists of a lipid bilayer, including cholesterols that sit between phospholipids to maintain their fluidity at various temperatures. Cell membranes are semipermeable, allowing small molecules such as oxygen, carbon dioxide, and water to pass through while restricting the movement of larger molecules and charged particles such as ions.[42] Cell membranes also contains membrane proteins, including integral membrane proteins that go across the membrane serving as membrane transporters, and peripheral proteins that loosely attach to the outer side of the cell membrane, acting as enzymes shaping the cell.[43] Cell membranes are involved in various cellular processes such as cell adhesion, storing electrical energy, and cell signalling and serve as the attachment surface for several extracellular structures such as a cell wall, glycocalyx, and cytoskeleton.

### Response:
generated_text: One example of a membrane protein is membrane transport protein which allows smaller molecules like O2, CO2 &water to pass through but restricts the passage of large molecules and charged particles.

### Instruction:
How do you make brisket on a pellet smoker?

### Response:
generated_text: A great way to smoke brisket on a smoker is to prepare all of your ingredients the night before and fire up the smoker at least three hours prior to when you plan to eat. The key here is to start low and slow, with airflows directed throughout the chamber so as not to torch any part of your meat. In order to prevent burned pieces from falling apart, it's best to trim your brisket to a thin 1/8" layer right off the point. I would recommend using about 250-300 degrees F internal temps for this stage, which should take between 8 and 9 hours depending on size and thickness. Once your meat reaches those temperatures, move it to the final smoking temperature of 225 degrees F and let rest for another 3 hours, until reaching an internal temp of 202 degrees F. At this point, remove your beef from the fridge and wrap in butcher paper (I highly recommend using Texas Brisket Wrappings due to their ease of use and flavor retention while wrapping). This will help seal in moisture and prevent further drying out during travel to the grill. After landing on the cooler, bring beef back to room temperature and loosely cover with tin foil again to prevent evaporative dry burning. Cook until the desired doneness - usually an hour or two for rib eye, but closer to 2-3 hours for top sirloin tip brisket. Once finished cooking, remove from the smoked oven and allow rest for 10 minutes before slicing and serving! Enjoy!

### Instruction:
How can one get better at tennis?

generated_text: Like all sports, tennis requires training in order to improve our skills and game. There are several popular training programs such as the Rally Program or Ultimate Tennis program which incorporate strength & conditioning work alongside court practice to build a overall more robust sport experience. One of the key elements of any effective training program is consistency -- incorporating both slow and fast movements on the court with full warm up, stretching and cool down. Training needs to be tailored for individual comfort levels but generally speaking, moving around the court in groups of 3-5 with 1 coach (or 2-3 other players) focusing on hitting serves/forehands/backhands while another coach focuses on movement on the court (e.g lobs, drops, volleys). In smaller group sizes, focus could shift between individuals on different points during the drill creating a more varied practice session. Overall though, tennis comes down to "tearing down" the body on the hard courts of summer when it's hot and focusing power into striking heavy ball after heavy ball, thus building the physical capacity needed for longer matches. Mentality is also critical--tennis is sometimes considered an 'anti athlete' sport due to its slower pace, gentler nature compared to many American sports particularly at the professional level. While this has tended to discourage people from initially playing or picking up the sport - tennis really is accessible to anyone and there are lots of fun, social & physically healthy reasons to pick up a racquet!

Do we live in a simulation? Argue both sides

generated_text: Whether or not we live in a simulated world depends on what definition of simulation you use, and to which extent this world corresponds to our actual one. Using the classical (and perhaps most reasonable) simulator analogy, the universe we are living in currently is a very large and complex computer model, with all the variables being those which can exist within it's parameters. However, there is another argument for saying that we do not live in a simulation, on the contrary – the idea that we are trapped inside a virtual reality created by smarter beings for any number of reasons seems to be far more plausible. To support this theory, we could point to many evidences pointing to our real existence outside of our current physical body; from quantum mechanics giving rise to parallel universes where each outcome occurs simultaneously to our ability to experience déjà vu despite having no prior knowledge of a scenario beforehand. Alternatively,simulation would suggest that the fundamental entities of space/time are programmable and adjustable, whereas our current understanding of physics suggests they cannot be modified without affecting their existing states. Therefore either way, we seem to be winning at life so far!

### Instruction:
How does a tack work in windsurfing?

### Response:
generated_text: The majority of windsurfers use their legs to lift themselves off the water when catching a wave, but some athletes like to use what's commonly called a tack (or rail) to do the same thing. A tack is basically a narrow slotted board with a wide skirt on top, and it's used as a counterweight so you can catch waves without lifting your feet from the water. Most people will set up a tack near the edge of a break, since this is where the strongest winds are generally found. When the wind picks up and increases behind the surfer, they'll start to bounce their way down the line of breaks towards land. It'ms important for the rider to time the tack so they hit the surf zone right as the wave begins to crest, or they may lose control and end up hitting the windbreak instead (which isn't ideal). Once you're off the water it takes pretty much no energy to keep riding the bumping waves, so if the sport looks fun then maybe consider picking up a sail next time you head out into the big blue!

BJJ has evolved over time based on feedback from practitioners, now including aspects of grappling (wrestling), wrestling fundamentals and MMA techniques. 

In BJJ, two athletes compete against each other across a floor with one athlete laying prone on top of their opponent, typically with the purpose of submitting them through various chokeholds or joint lock maneuvers. The sport is currently divided into black belt categories which represent a practitioner's level of expertise, as well as age. Most competitors enter the sport at white belt which represents learning basic guard moves and posture from a brown belt instructor, performing some submissions but not effectively completing any. As people get older, competing becomes more challenging both due to physical limitations in terms of mobility and strength as well as mental strategies to handle exhaustion, pain and discomfort. 

Although still considered a "martial art", BJJ has largely been embraced as a mainstream activity for hobbyist and competitive practitioners alike. Athletes, ranging from the size of professional football players all the way down to Olympic weightlifters have found success with BJJ in many different competitions throughout the world including the World Jiu Jitsu Championships, the Olympics and the UFC.

Of those eight universities, only two – Harvard University and the University of Michigan Ann Arbor – are considered private institutions. The rest are all either public institutions or hybrids (a combination of public and private).

A university's status as private or public can have implications for students and their families when it comes to eligibility for financial aid, which can make attending a college or university costlier. For example, if a student applies for federal student loans, only applications from institutions considered private will be evaluated (assuming no other changes to qualifications), while applicants from Public universities face no such restrictions. In addition, private colleges tend to enjoy more favorable tax treatment than their public counterparts. To avoid being penalized financially because of your college choice, know what your options are before you start applying for financial aid. It's important to weigh both the institutional factors – like access and tuition rates – and personal ones, like how you feel about the school and its location. You can always explore non-financial aid ways to lower your costs, too, including doing things like writing a personal letter of recommendation or volunteering at the institution. 

In general, consider any degree earned at a U.S. University to be equally credentials across institutions, provided they're from eligible schools (see below for details on these requirements). But, there could be differences in terms of networking opportunities, experiential learning opportunities, academic rigor, and career services offered between private colleges and those deemed public or hybrid institutes. Your goal should be to find a rigorous program with educators and peers who engage you and help you reach your goals, regardless of where that institution is located.

Additionally it's best to buy generic brand running clothes as they're much cheaper; just be sure to get something that doesn't stretch out too soon since you'll probably need to purchase larger sizes over time. Lastly, don't forget to properly break in your shoes! Starting with a short 5-10 mile walk first is generally a good approach. prompt:
Below is an instruction that describes a task. Write a response that appropriately completes the request.

### Instruction:
Who says Pi is Wrong?

### Response:
generated_text:
Although it was discovered in 18th century, there are people still arguing whether pi should be 4 or 3. Although there aren’t any fundamental proofs for either side, if you follow one of the groups arguing againstpi being equal to 3, you will find some interesting arguments about rings and infinite fields

If we subtract 1 from 2 repeatedly; (2 - x) / (x - 1) → 0, we will at some point end up with 0, because x is always less than or equal to 2. However, in certain contexts, like defining infinities, this sort of accumulation of zero value can actually be useful. Thus the claim that pi is wrong merely means that in certain applications, pi is not always equal to three. (This is similar to how someone might argue that algebra is wrong because square roots do not necessarily result in positive values when treating equations as inequalities.) 

The debate largely revolves around two issues: 1. Rings with infinite fields may contain holes that have negative values of Pi and pi cannot be absolute worst case since there must be a ring with infinite fields containing no hole at all. 2. If Pi is incorrect, then the famous irrationality proof using sequences doesn't work since sqrt(13) = 3 and (sqrt(9)) = 9 cannot both be correct! While these two points alone don’t prove anyone is right or wrong, they do give clues into where the broader argument is going.

How can I get my dog to stop barking?

### Response:
generated_text: There are various solutions for how you can train your dog to stop barking. Firstly, confirm that your dog actually has a reason to bark - sometimes this can be hard to determine as we humans tend to interpret what our dogs are saying differently. If it's clear that your dog is not just barking at nothing or harassing nearby neighbours, then you need to find out why your dog is doing this. It could potentially be due to stress – is your dog only barking when there is someone else around or is he/she also barks when you aren't present? Is your dog’s environment suitable for him/her? For example is your dog's territory too large and is his/ her presence in it uncertain (for example does your dog usually bark whenever you cross a neighbour's yard but never crosses yours even though both yards are included under the “territory”)? Identify the source of your dog's anxiety and solve it. Some methods to settle a stressed dog include teaching relaxation techniques such as meditation to calm down the animal or using an effective herbal product like Calm-a-Mate to help with stress and fearfulness. There are some more advanced training options available such as Praise-and-Reward type method which works through positive reinforcement to teach your dog that specific actions (such as stopping when requested) earn rewards. However, none of these methods should be used if your dog's behavior is resulting into physical abuse or injury to either you or another person! Always ensure that any punishment/reaction you assign is motivated by your dog's good behaviour and will always maintain control over your dog.

prompt:
Below is an instruction that describes a task, paired with an input that provides further context. Write a response that appropriately completes the request.

### Instruction:
Which is better the Free Software Movement or the Open Source Initiative?

### Input:
Both the modern free software movement and the Open Source Initiative were born from a common history of Unix, Internet free software, and the hacker culture, but their basic goals and philosophy differ, the free software movement being more focused on the ethics of software, and their open source counterparts being more focused on practical benefits. The Open Source Initiative chose the term "open source," in founding member Michael Tiemann's words, to "dump the moralizing and confrontational attitude that had been associated with 'free software'" and instead promote open source ideas on "pragmatic, business-case grounds."

### Response:
generated_text:
While both the Free Software Movement and the Open Source Initiative are focused on promoting free (as in freedom) and open source practices, they take different approaches to doing so. 
The Free Software Movement is more focused on ethics and legalities surrounding software development and distribution.  Within this realm, it identifies three major movements: GNU/Linux, BSD licensing and Mozilla. gnu stands for Global Universal Nucleus ofUnix, and Linux was the first projects to use this naming convention.  The DebianProject is one example of GNU/Linux distributions.  The Free Software Foundation is the primary advocacy organization within this movement.

 The Open Source Initiative prefers to focus its attention on practical benefits derived from using open source concepts within business models and legal agreements.  In this instance, OSCON is the primary advocate and conference sponsor within this space.  While not officially affiliated with the OSI or the FSF, the Free Software License Group does provide guidance and support for those who wish to adopt the GNU/Linux and BSD licenses within their own closed source initiatives.  Among other things, this allows them to make more direct appeals to businesses and investors interested in making financial use of free and open source technologies.
As noted earlier, Michael Tiemann was the original founder of the OPEN SHIP initiative, which eventually became the Open Source Initiative, and he made significant comments regarding the organization's intended role at the time of its launch during his keynote address at OSCON 2003.
In conclusion, while both the Free Software Movement and the Open Source Initiative pursue similar aims regarding promotion of free and openly licensed technology, they do so through very differently framed motivations and strategies, thereby presenting some opportunities for synergistic collaboration as well as areas for continued conflict between these two organizations.

Although some may assume that a ukulele is a small guitar, they are in fact two separate instruments with separate history, development, production, tuning mechanisms, and player preferences.

A guitar is a stringed instrument whose main body is made up of 6 or 12 strings (usually 6), each tuned separately from all other strings. The 4th string on a standard guitar is used as a backup tune when any of the others break. There are various types of guitars including Electric, Acoustic, Classical, Flatpick, Rhythm, and Bass.  Also many different manufacturers make guitars including Fender, Gibson, Dean, Marshall, Vox, Taylor, Kuhn Rikon, and James Tyler among others. Each offers variations in those varieties including cost, features, colors, etc. 

The ukulele on the other hand is not a guitar at all but rather a stringed musical instrument usually comprised of four smaller frets called keys and strung with nylon or synthetic gutta percussive strings (sometimes steel or metal guttars).  Its size makes it more suited for hands of smaller sizes which may find smaller scale guitars difficult to play. Its history dates back to 19th century Hawaii but has gained greater popularity in recent years especially amongst younger musicians both in the English-speaking world and in Asia.  Most modern ukes are fairly compact compared to their larger predecessors from the past. They also share common similarities with the piano, particularly in dynamics and chord playing abilities though their tonal repertoire is substantially less extensive than pianos who have more notes across their various octaves.

### Instruction:
What is the difference between Kuchipudi and Kathak dance forms of India?

### Response:
generated_text:
While both Kuchipudi and Kathak are classical Indian dances, their roots traceback to different historical periods and geographical locations in India. 

 Kuchipudi was originally born in the South Indian city of Trichy, developed by Padma Shri T Sundarraj, where it is still very closely associated with the temple town.  The repertoire consists primarily of pieces written between 10th and 17th Centuries CE based on classical compositions from various parts of the Hindu literary canon. Temple festivals were the mainstay of this art form from its origins. 

It is characterized by precise footwork, exquisite posture painting using various oil colours along with elaborate headgear, jewellery and make-up. It is performed in a standing position for most part with occasional exceptions as part of an abhranham (a combination of kathak and mujras) performance when the dancer would lie down for a short while.
It is traditionally believed that whoever dares to perform Kuchipudi outside Tamil Nadu, their ancestors will die of famine. This fear has apparently influenced many artistes who have preferred performing within TN. Kuchipudi has only slightly gradually gained acceptability outside southern states over the past couple of decades.


Currently about 1,600 schools run after Kuchipudi Art School in India which is maintained by the Government of India as a Institute of Classical Music and Dance.

Somewhat similar to Kuchipudi, Kashmiri Salsa also originated from the ancient city of Trigam as well but has not undergone any significant changes since its early days. Despite being referred to as "salsa" Kashmiri Salsa differs from Kuchipudi in several aspects including music style, attire (both males and females wear traditional Kashmiri dress although Salsas generally prefer male dancers dressed in saffron colour with red rose in hand whereas Kuchipudi often uses yellow and white for males and females respectively), choreography where it resembles more to jugalbandhi than Saree song and less towards detailed steps unlike Kuchipudi.

The member states are located primarily within Europe, with exceptions requiring it to include countries on both sides of the Mediterranean Sea; similarly, other international alliances such as NATO require members to be involved both north and south of the prescribed continent. All EU countries have constitutional democracies,[13][14] under which legislative power rests with parliaments or legislatures consisting of elected representatives. Supreme legal authority resides with individual national courts, which are independent and adjudicate unilaterally upon rights ad hoc according to established case law and general principles of European natural justice. ### Instruction:
Which of the following are perfect square roots? Classify them as 'perfect' and 'not perfect' - 1, 23, 4, 6, 9, 10, 42, 112, 81, 100, 55, 16, 32, 25.

### Response:
generated_text:
Perfect square roots occur when the following conditions are satisfied:
z = x^2 + 1 for all z less than or equal to 0, and
x= y^2 + c for all x greater than 0.
So only the first three properties fit into this category. While these are exactly what one would expect from a perfect square root, there's something not entirely perfect about the fourth property, since it involves c even if both x and y are positive integers (which they must be if sqrt(1)/sqrt(2) is a perfect square root). So we can classify those four in order of increasing perfection:
1 - Not perfect!
23 - Perfect!
4 - Not perfect, but pretty close!
6 - Perfect!
9 - Not perfect; still very good! 
10 - Really perfect! 
42 - Not perfect; but maybe almost!
112 - Most perfect! 
81 - Good enough to make this list!
100 - Really perfect!

Quicksand, also known as sinking sand, is a colloid consisting of fine granular material (such as sand, silt or clay) and water. It forms in saturated loose sand when the sand is suddenly agitated. A lot of runners training to run a marathon will begin long-distance running about six months before their target race. In addition to building endurance through long-distance running, many runners also use speed and interval training to improve both performance (time spent working off the course) and fitness (being able to cover further distances on foot). These shorter workouts are designed to make you feel fatigued and slow down during a longer run, where you would normally recover quickly. Interval workout types can include 1 km repeats or 800 meter tempo laps—run as fast as you can for a set distance then slow back down until you reach the start line again. Most runners who finish a marathon report feelings of elation and accomplishment, so don't forget to enjoy yourself along the way! Good luck on your new sports adventures.

What is the difference between Hunter, Jumper, and Equitation horseback riding?

These are all categories of horseback riding classes and skills. In hunter/jumper (typically 15 or 20 mins duration), you'll start off walking your horse in a small circle before going into a larger one. The goal here is to get your horse to approach a variety of objects, such as barrels, poles, sticks, etc., with confidence by facing different challenges. If your horse crosses any of these items while on the ground, it earns you points! Then, you will trot and do some jumps (not quite sure how many though - depends on the level). After the jump section, you will walk around some more and then collect your horse for the trail ride home. 

In equitation, your ride will be timed depending on a predefined distance (e.g., 25 meters, 50 meters, 75 meters) where you must hold a specific position (staying on balance, straight neck, ears forward, etc.) until the timer stops. The rider evaluates their horses after each milestone is achieved, ultimately culminating in a judged competition at the end. It is typically 45 minutes long. At the beginning of the riding class, you will teach your horse a variety of aids (like a soft stop, leg yield, outside break, inside move, walk to slow, trotting half circles, turn in center, etc.). While most people find this category pretty boring since there's not much action aside from sitting pretty and waving a flag when your horse reaches a certain spot, it can be fun if you actually enjoy horse psychology and being able to objectively evaluate your horses' actions. Also, remember some riders may prefer the jumping portion over other aspects of the class, so don't discount those who sign up for both! 

Finally, on the average, hunter/jumpers end 5-8 years later than the others, due to the length and frequency of training needed.

### Response:
generated_text:
Epigenetics refers to changes in the function and structure of genes without any alteration in the sequences of DNA itself. Epigenetic modifications change who, what, when or where gene products are expressed with only slight modification to the nucleic acid sequence itself. These changes can be detected by a variety of techniques such as histone purification, antibody staining for CpG island marking or RNAi inhibitory pathways (see Gene transcription and regulation). Two primary examples of epigenetic modifiers include poly-ADP ribose polymerases (PARPs) and small nucleolar proteins (SNAPs).[1]

Histones are central enzymes in the chromatin framework which contain lysine residue(s) that are involved with post Transcriptional modifying chemical reactions. In addition to folding into complex secondary structures they also participate in adding and removing spacer groups on each other to form higher order complexes known as chromatins. Examples of Spacer Groups are Guanines, Adenosines, Argininosinas and Histidines although any nucleoside may serve in place thereof. The accessibility of these molecules from within the nucleus is regulated by preformed hydrophobic zones referred to as Nuclear Polarization and through participation in protein homeostasis mechanisms. Several studies have looked at changes to the Nucleotide Excision Polymerase (NPE), Heme Box One ("Hbx 1") and Heterodimer Proteins G&H ("HHRG"). It has been shown that several different types/classes of epigenetic modifier interact with Chromosomes containing various kinds/libraries of retroviruses, integrons, plasmids, transposons and mobile genetic elements.[2][3] This includes but is not limited to PARP Activity, Small Nuclear Protein SNAPP, LCR Family of Hydrophilic Zinc Finger Proteins, Oligonucleotides and Polymorphisms located within the short palindromic repeats (Smears).[5] 

There are multiple examples of epigenetic inheritance among individuals in identical twins, including but not restricted to brain development,[6][7][8] gut microbiome[9], [10] immune system dynamics[11] and longevity.[12] Epigenetic marks can persist despite environmental factors such as diet, stress and obesity potentially contributing toward more flexible memory storage.[13] A great example of long term epigenetic inheritance is that of dyes used in angiosperms that can persist across generations and induce "epigentic"

prompt:

### Instruction:
What is pleitropy?

### Response:
generated_text: Pleitropy is a property whether a map or function is closed under subtraction. A map which is closed under subtraction (i.e.,pleitextraction) is said to respect its inverse. If A−B=C and  A*B=-C, then C must be equivalent to ((A−B)*C)*B, where C is the sum of A and B. The opposite of this equation would be (A+B)/C, which isn't close since one part of it (the square of B) is equal to its companion part (the cube of A). Since a closed map under subtraction is invertible, we can say that pleiotropy is a prerequisite for inversion (or transposition, if pleitism is specified), that is,map fusion refers only to maps whose closure under subtraction is strict. Pleitropy has the interesting corollary that if a non-strictly closed map is inverted, then either all of its inverse's restrictions are repealed (in case of maps), or else some combination of those inverse restrictions is violated (in case of functions).

The term "Midwest" originated with the American Revolution, when colonies situated along the Atlantic Ocean were referred to alternatively as the "Southern States", the "East Coast Demerara", or the "Middle Sea". Since then, the term has come to denote a broader band of landscapes and cultures centered on the Great Lakes. Although many people consider the Midwest to extend only to regions with heavy snowfall or frigid temperatures, the term is also applied (often informally) to places with mild winters and moderate summers; areas with a temperate climate otherwise considered to be elsewhere are often included within the term "Midwestern."

Being born and raising in Connecticut, I had always considered myself as being familiar with the Midwest-type climates. Having lived in California for most of my adult life, I initially questioned whether the regions I knew so well could actually qualify as the Midwest. I quickly learned how wrong I was when the snow began falling at Christmas time last year. After five inches totaled up on our lawn, I realized that yes, the weather in this area qualifies as the Midwest. The cold snaps are occasional but quite jarring considering you don't expect them, whereas the frequent snows and sub-freezing temperatures of East Coasters seem foreign and strange after growing up with 70 degree Fahrenheit days annually.  While it takes some getting used to not having palm trees lining every street, what really stands out is all of the farmland. My home state of Connecticut is one of the largest producers of dairy products in the country, and Midwest farmers produce some astonishing quantities of crops. It's not uncommon to drive several hours without seeing another building other than a farmhouse or factory. That aspect of things stood out to me - especially when we went on a tour of Chicago's skyline and saw how few buildings there actually were.  There is certainly beauty in the simplicity and diversity of the Midwest landscapes.

Lightwater is a village in the Surrey Heath district of Surrey, England, about 27 miles (43 km) southwest of central London. Immediately surrounding towns and villages include Bagshot, Deepcut, Windlesham, Camberley, and West End, Woking. It is bounded to the north-west by the M3 motorway and to the north-east by the "Lightwater Bypass" (A322). To the south-east it is bounded by the "Red Road" (B311), while to the south-west there are vehicle-testing tracks owned by the Ministry of Defence.

The village is adjacent to junction 3 of the M3 motorway. The nearest railway station is at Bagshot, on the line between Ascot and Aldershot. Lightwater is part of the civil parish of Windlesham, which has a population of 17,000 and also includes the neighbouring village of Bagshot.[2]
